title: Europe’s Rearmament Is Hardening
text:
Europe’s Rearmament Is Hardening

August Gudmundsson

Gavekal Research | 9 Jul 2026

After a chaotic day in Ankara, Nato leaders appeared to reach a consensus, recommitting to spend 5% of GDP on defense. While attention centered on US president Donald Trump’s threats to restart bombing of Iran, the summit will likely be remembered for cementing Europe’s shift toward rearmament. The question now is whether that buildup happens quickly through purchases of US weapons systems or more gradually as Europe rebuilds its own military-industrial complex.Last year, European defense contractor stocks were re-rated on expectations of a domestically driven rearmament, particularly as the US and Europe appeared to be heading toward a rupture in the transatlantic alliance. This year, however, the MSCI Europe defense benchmark is down -6.7% from its January peak as investors have begun to doubt Europe’s fiscal capacity to deliver on its spending promises. Concerns have also grown that non-military expenditures will be bundled into headline defense spending.
